preamble: The transformation of a rhotic sound into a voiceless alveolar stop is a form of fortition. It occurs in specific phonetic environments, such as in emphatic speech, in the formation of emphatic consonants in Semitic languages, or in certain dissimilation processes where a liquid becomes a stop to increase contrast.

examples.note: In some Arabic dialects, the liquid /r/ may be replaced or reinforced by [t] in emphatic or reduplicated forms to create contrast.

sources: Holes, C. (2004). Modern Arabic: Structures, Functions, and Varieties.
Watson, J. C. (2002). The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic.
